speaker: interviewer
text: We're speaking with Dr. Zev Kohn, in New York City. And this is tape number three.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, so we are in a town in the middle of a desert. And there was starvation, disease, and so forth. Now, something that I should mention here is that when my father was in the hospital with typhoid fever, there was a quarantine there. And first of all, they were starving him. Because they were afraid that he may perforate his bowels if he's eating. And then they didn't want anybody to come near him. Because they were afraid, in some way, the disease may be spread elsewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a guard there that was watching. But I was a little kid. I would sneak by the window. And I would actually time the guard, how long it would take him to run to chase me away. And I would drop a little bit of these Hosbach flatbreads for my father so he can eat something.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time, I had occasion to see, at that time, I was 11 years old, and I think I am not exaggerating that I was just about the youngest child that survived from that-- from that group of people. The rest died. The girl that was with us, she died in Siberia, also from a streptococcal infection. She was my age. And then there was a little kid, maybe three or four years old, also from one of these families that were staying in our tent. And they brought him to the hospital. But the so-called hospital, what they would do, they would line up these dying kids by the wall in sunshine. And they would just die there. So-- so that kid died. And when the things-- things were quite bad and everybody was sick, then I, being more resilient to the extent that I was 11 years old, I used to go on the market and-- and I sort of tried to get the best deal out of every watermelon or every bunch of grapes that I would buy from these Hosbach peasants. Anyway, just short of all of us, actually, going bye-bye, so to speak, to the other world, the transport came. And they brought us to the-- to the-- to the-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] --a port on the Caspian Sea. We went on a ship. And again, it was a long climb. We're in-- in-- in desert conditions. There was no water there. And I remember my mother gave her watch to somebody for a glass of water. And there was, again, the Jews were not taken by the Poles. But there was one guy sneaking by. And he says, let me carry your-- your luggage. And I will-- can sneak on the ship. And he did. On the ship, everybody had diarrheas. It was terrible. But the point is, that by that time, I already had my shakes of malaria. And I was shaking just about everyday. And by the time we landed in Pahlavi, which was already, so to speak, resurrection, it was a little beach in Persia. And for the first time, I went to the fence and some Persian sold us eggs. And I think, it was the first egg I have seen, maybe, in two years or so. And we, between the three of us, we had 18 eggs. And I remember that. And malaria was shaking me. But then we got medicine for-- for malaria. And my father was still very sick. But before they transported him as a patient, he came to our tent, left us some money. So we still had food. And we were actually started coming to-- to ourselves. And then we came to Tehran. I'm almost towards the end, I think, of the whole saga. In Tehran, there were-- we lived in Polish refugee camps, with Poles. And I remember there was a-- a messenger that came out from occupied Poland, and was telling the conditions in Poland at that time. And that that must have been the year '42, end of '42. That's when the transports in Poland were going to Auschwitz and Treblinka, in full speed. And he was saying how terrible it was. The Germans grabbed two or three peasant women on the railroad station. And-- and shot them. Because they thought they were Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my mother then tells-- told me-- I was a little kid. After that she tells me, what did he say? He said, that they got two Poles. And they shot them because they thought they were Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: That means they are killing all the Jews there. She understood the-- the extent of the-- of what is going on, just from the-- what the guy didn't want to say, that the Jews were not even mentioned in his report. All he was talking, was he was he was talking about the Poles. And then one day, my mother was doing her wash in sink apparently not far, of all places, of a church on Sunday. And a Polish policeman came and brutalized her, beat her up. She came crying. And then about myself, we couldn't go to school. It was difficult to go [? one-by-one ?] to school because the Poles were-- they're ganging up and beating us. So there was one stronger kid. His name was Perelmutter, and he would protect us. There was a whole bunch of us going to school together. One day, I went to latrine. Because that's where we were going. There were no bathrooms. There was no running water. It Was a latrine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were ditches, long ditches, with planks across. And of course, there was malnutrition and all this. So people were still going, you know, with diarrheas and such. And I go. I went there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was 12 years old. And four or five of them came to me and says, you goddamn Jew, either you stop dropping your Jewish shit in-- in this Polish latrine, or we'll drown you in this ditch right away. It was full, of course, of liquid shit. And it was four of them. And I was 12-years-old. And they said, we're going to count now. We're going to count to 10. If you don't clear this place by 10, you will be swimming in-- in this dirt.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the 10 gave me time to figure out what to do. And I took aim. They were standing-- standing in-- a semicircle around me. I took aim at the guy that was standing to my extreme left.

speaker: interviewee
text: I sort of mentally measured the distance, took a good look how he's standing. And by the time they said 9, I kick him with all my force in his balls. And the guy just cried out in agony. It came as a complete surprise to the whole bunch of them. That gave me enough time to pack up and leave in a hurry. And they never found me. And I got my satisfaction. And up to this date, I'm very proud that they didn't get away with this. I could have left quietly, but I didn't. The other thing is from-- that goes back to my memory, I remember when we were still in Tehran. And my father, in other words, in Persia, in a refugee camp. Because my father was then in good health. And I came to accompany him. His transport was going to Iraq, through Iraq, to then Palestine. And I came to accompany him. It was some old factory, completely empty, big-- big structures. Machinery was gone. That's where this transport was assembling. And I came-- maybe it was raining. I came with my hat on. And another Polish officer turns-- turns to me and says, look, you Jew, you take your hat off, you are quote, unquote, 'in a Christian building.' Well, it wasn't Christian. It was for anything. It was Muslim, or-- or-- or whatever it was, or to whoever the factory belonged.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I looked at my father. What is going on here? My father nodded his head.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, you-- in other words, my father told me to-- to listen what the guy is saying. And I did take my hat off. It's just another little thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where are we now? We are now in-- in Persia, with Polish anti-Semitism. And that's basically it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the Jews got separated in Persia. Somebody from Joint came. And somebody from Jewish Agency came. And-- and there were about 3,000 of us. And these 3,000, through special agreement, again, between the British and the-- the British and the Polish government. Not the Polish government. I don't know who. Anyway, the-- the man-- the-- the British government allowed these 3,000 Jews to come from Tehran on a transport and go to Palestine. And we went through Suez. And we arrived in Palestine. This, incidentally, [? began ?] [? travel ?] [? as ?] my father through Iraq, and also to Palestine. And he dropped out from the Polish Army and went into underground. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] Then in Israel, we started a new life. Now, looking backwards, in terms of numbers, the 3,000 Jews that came from Persia, I think they were the only Jews, maybe few hundred here and there, that came to Palestine throughout the war. '30-- in 1939, when we left Poland and went into Russia, there was 300,000 Jews that escaped that way from Germans. But then a lot of them, prior to '41, returned back to-- to-- to Germany, or to Warsaw or to German-occupied areas, since the life under Soviet rule wasn't that good either. In other words, the shipments to Siberia and such scared some people. And they returned to Germany. And obviously, most of them perished. And these that got out from Russia, there were just 3,000 of us. And the rest stayed in-- in-- in Russia, whatever the correct number is. And-- and returned-- some of them returned to Poland. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's basically the end of it. I don't know whether I should go to my life in-- in-- in pre-Israel Palestine and Israel itself. Should I or shouldn't I? I'd-- I'd like to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Only briefly.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd-- I'd like to maybe go-- go back to Persia. Did the camp have a specific name? The refugee camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just numbers. And camp number one, number two, and number three. They were long barracks, constructed specifically for this-- for this population.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there many Jewish in the [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's what it was, the 3,000. Amongst all the camps, that was 3,000 Jews. Because the Poles were not taking the Jews out of Russia. It was the Polish government that was organizing the exodus from Russia. And they were not taking Jews. So there was a Jewish orphanage that came. These were my friends at that time, because they were my age. But it was Jewish orphanage. The rest, I don't think, was more than 3,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have contact with the population outside the refugee camp, with the Persians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, not at all. I mean, here and there, just to say hello and we didn't have language to communicate with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What was your first reaction, arriving in Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Couldn't understand how everybody on the street is a Jew. I just couldn't-- I couldn't get over this. I couldn't get over this. Everybody a Jew? [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] It was a different ballgame already. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Ask me questions now, if you want some other general points. I'll be glad to, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are agencies in Palestine that helped you become established there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They put us-- they put us up in an old whorehouse. There used to be a whorehouse for the British military. [NON-ENGLISH], but it's true. And the police-- the military police closed the whorehouse and gave us-- you can imagine what we got, yeah. We got two or three families to a room. But that's just few months. And then we got [NON-ENGLISH]. And we-- we-- Then my father left the Polish Army and the whole thing became different. Then I joined Haganah. And then was the war of liberation of '48, which I was still 17 when-- when the war broke out. But I participated when I was 17, in-- in the underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it that you first learned about the full extent of what had happened in-- in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now that's another story. I was initially very angry, angry at Jews, that they didn't tell me, in Israel, the extent of the Holocaust. In other words, they didn't spell it out for me. In our family, my parents were heartbroken. They obviously understood it. But nobody yet-- nobody could yet put it in words or in numbers, or in attitudes. It is just that there was some-- when my father got old-- there was some woman coming, taking care of him. And she was telling the story to my mother that they were leading them to the ditches to be shot. And she heard the shooting, and she just escaped. I-- I was, in 1946-- in other words, before Israel, I came to. But after the war already, after '45, I was with somebody on a kibbutz, that he was telling the story, that how the Germans were evacuating their concentration camps towards the end of the war, and how he survived that. At that time, I was 16. And my perception was, well, that was his tough luck that he was where-- where he was. And had he been here in Israel, as I was, then it wouldn't have happened to him. But I couldn't quite understand the mechanics of it, the mechanics, that I started to pick up the full understanding of Holocaust only on my own, when I was fully grown up, already with children. I would go into every detail of every locality, every date in-- in-- in Warsaw, as a place. I would go into details how different members of my family perished. [CRYING] Um, to be-- to be specific, for instance-- and then, when my older son, I think, had bar mitzvah, and the whole [NON-ENGLISH] came. They from Sweden. They came from Denmark. They came from all over the world to his bar mitzvah. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: We sit Kaddish for them, one by one.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] One by one, saying how-- how they perished. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] There-- my father-- my father's two brother, my mother's sisters, my cousins. [CRYING] And that's it. The full realization didn't come during my years in Israel. I-- I came here for-- for post-doctoral training. I came in '57. Up to that time in '57, at least in Israel, there was no understanding at all-- at least on my level-- of Holocaust. Maybe Ben-Gurion understood it, and people like him. Whether it was done intentionally or not, I don't know. I suspect that part of this was to protect the Israeli public from the extent of the disaster. I don't know, with this kind of psychological trauma, whether people can go and fight for their survival. If you would get really in acute stage into the terrible burden of-- of the-- of the disaster. And now-- now we understand it better. And now we also misunderstand it better. Because the things that were understood then are not understood now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think you were treated differently in Israel because you were a-- a European refugee?

speaker: interviewee
text: Than who?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a different status than-- than the people who were there already?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were no other-- except for European refugees, there were no other refugees. The Iraqi Jews started coming only in 1952. The American Jews, a little bit later. There were no-- I don't have any-- any-- any comments on this. And I think any-- if there are any comments, they are totally out of place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- I even don't know what you're referring to, European versus Eskimos or European versus Arabs, or Jews versus Martians? Who versus what? It's one people. OK. Can you describe your activities with the Haganah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah. It was-- we were living in Holon. And it was on the southern border of Jaffa. And Etzel, in other words, Irgun, was attacking from the north, from Tel Aviv. They were penetrating into Jaffa. And we were just holding-- holding a line in the south. Because you know, you would wake up in the morning and there were always a few bullets in the door of-- of-- for instance, my father's office. The shooting at night was going all the time. And the Arabs were coming. And we were, at night, patrolling the area and making sure that they won't come. Once during a patrol, we are-- there are sand dunes, or were sand dunes at that time. I would come down the sand dunes. And the Arabs were sitting in the bushes at the bottom of the hill. I heard the guy putting the-- the bullet in-- in-- in the-- cocking his-- his-- his gun. They opened fire. We were in the middle of the-- of the-- but these things were nothing. Important people were killed too. Because we were sometimes operating out of little bunkers. And the Arabs would aim the-- their machine guns into the slits-- you call it slit-- in the bunker. and the bullets started. They would hear someone and they would shoot. And they would kill people. There were some casualties.

speaker: interviewer
text: And once I remember a Davidka came. You know what Davidka is? Davidka was a product of Jewish ingenuity, a lot of wishful thinking, and a little bit of gunpowder. It was-- how you call it? Not a gun, but the-- the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, it was the only thing that we had at that time to fire at the Arabs. The point was, that sometimes it was firing on the Arabs. Sometimes it was exploding on-- on the launching pad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a long cord, like, 30 feet long. And we were hiding behind the building and pulling on the string. And-- but when it was landing in Jaffa, it was making a big boom.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] It was very scary to the Arabs. And that was the size of it. I didn't commit any acts of bravery or any exploits. If anything, I was a kid. I was the youngest in this whole outfit.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what facilitated your coming to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: I finished medical school in-- in Israel. And I-- we didn't have, at that time yet, training programs in surgery. In other words, I would have to go start-- start working in the hospital and then either-- whatever. There was no training program. It would depend on the-- on the goodwill of the guy that wanted to pass some cases to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came for training. And I trained in-- in a good hospital here. And-- and that is basically how I stayed. Was it difficult to make the transition?

speaker: interviewee
text: Between Israel? Um-- culturally, maybe, yes. But otherwise, I mean, you have all this-- when I came here again, you know, you were asking how did I perceive Israel when I came to Israel. If the question is how I perceived New York, it was full of these kosher delicatessens. And, you know, at that time, the Jews were still living in the Bronx. And I-- my first stop in-- was in the Bronx. I-- they grabbed me in the morning to daven, because they didn't have a minyan. I mean, this was just like a-- another gefilte fish, so to speak. It was not-- not much different. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't go into these things now.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And you're-- you're-- what kind of physician did you become?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm a surgeon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about your children? Or your child?

speaker: interviewee
text: My children, I have four kids, two boys, two girls. All are doing very well. Then again, I wouldn't go past that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you give us their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ela, Daniel, Ezra, and Shulamite.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And your grandchild?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my grandchild is a little girl. I have only one. And I love her very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What do you think having this document, your story on tape, what do you think that will mean to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: To my children?

speaker: interviewer
text: And their children.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. You know, I really don't know these things. I really don't know. One thing is what we want to believe in, and the other is what the world brings. And I haven't lived long enough to-- to see the difference between the reality and the world the way it presents itself. The world is not organized by anybody. The world has its own momentum. Anybody that tries to organize the world is just doing a lot of-- creating a lot of problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I have no idea. All I know, that I'm here, now. And I am putting on record what I lived through.

speaker: interviewee
text: What other people do with this, I don't know. And I don't-- I can't even fathom what they conceivably could or could not do with this.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I want to thank you very much for allowing us to do this. It's an important document that we've created.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I want to thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.
